text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 11 August 2020 

Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist,  

Samuel Bennett, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: S902174 NHS No. 7482193650  
19 Brookfield Gardens, London, SE15 2QL

Thank you for asking me to review your patient in our neurology clinic. He attended with his partner and provided a clear account of recent events. We also had access to your referral note and the community pharmacy dispensing history. Long-term antiseizure therapy with bone-health monitoring is recorded elsewhere; I will not duplicate those details here but have noted they are ongoing. 

He described an initial period of improvement after we introduced Levetiracetam at the last review. He remained seizure-free for 5 months after starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, before experiencing a generalised tonic-clonic seizure 2 Tuesdays ago, preceded by a cluster of absences. The absences were described as brief pauses with loss of awareness, lasting 10–20 seconds, occurring several times across that morning. The subsequent convulsion was witnessed by his partner, with bilateral tonic then clonic activity lasting approximately 90 seconds, tongue biting, and postictal confusion for around 45 minutes. There was no reported head injury or status epilepticus. He has otherwise felt well, with regular sleep and no intercurrent illness. He denies alcohol or recreational drug use and reports full adherence to medication. 

Potential precipitants discussed included sleep disruption the weekend prior due to travel, and a missed lunch on the day of the event. He denies intercurrent infection or new medications. There have been no recent episodes at night according to his partner, and no injuries since the event. He is currently not driving and is aware of DVLA guidance. 

Neurological examination in clinic today was unremarkable. Cardiovascular and general examination were normal. There were no focal deficits and gait was steady. Cognitive screening was normal for age. He reports occasional irritability since starting Levetiracetam, without low mood, suicidality, or psychotic features; he prefers to continue and monitor this for now. 

Plan and actions today:
- Continue Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily for now, with emphasis on adherence and sleep hygiene. He has an adequate supply and a blister-pack arrangement via his pharmacy.
- Safety counselling reinforced: seizure first aid, bathing/showering precautions, and avoidance of working at heights or near open water.
- We discussed the option of dose optimisation if further events occur; he prefers not to change today given the preceding 5-month remission and single breakthrough event with identifiable stressors.
- Arrange routine bloods locally (U&E, LFT, FBC) and vitamin D as part of his ongoing bone-health monitoring pathway, which is documented separately.
- Request copy of prior EEG/MRI from your records and radiology to consolidate his file.
- Provide a seizure diary and triggers checklist; his partner will help with documentation of any further absences.
- DVLA advice reiterated. He understands to inform DVLA as applicable and to refrain from driving in line with guidance after a convulsive seizure.

Follow-up: I have arranged a telephone review in 6 weeks, with an earlier face-to-face appointment if there is any further event or concern. He and his partner know how to contact our epilepsy nurse specialist for interim advice. 

Many thanks for your referral and ongoing care. Please let me know if there is additional information from primary care that would help, including vaccination status or any intercurrent illness around the time of the breakthrough seizure. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
- The letter states he was seizure-free for 5 months after starting levetiracetam, then had a breakthrough event 2 Tuesdays ago consisting of a cluster of absences that morning followed by one generalised tonic-clonic seizure.
- He is not seizure-free for ≥6 months, so we must calculate frequency.
- Count the cluster as one seizure event (exact number of absences within the cluster is not specified), plus one generalised tonic-clonic seizure = 2 events.
- Denominator: the explicitly stated period is 5 months of remission before the event, so use 5 months.
- Final: 2 seizures per 5 months.
seizure_frequency_number: He remained seizure-free for 5 months after starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, before experiencing a generalised tonic-clonic seizure 2 Tuesdays ago, preceded by a cluster of absences.
